title: scenario_whaling
description: A highly targeted whaling attack impersonates a board member to pressure the CFO into authorising a fraudulent wire transfer.

timeline.title: Urgent Email to CFO
timeline.description: The CFO receives an email appearing to be from the CEO demanding an urgent confidential wire transfer.

timeline.title: Pressure Escalates
timeline.description: A follow-up email stresses secrecy and a tight deadline, urging the CFO to bypass normal approval channels.

timeline.title: Verification Attempt
timeline.description: CFO pauses and attempts to verify the request through an independent channel.

timeline.title: Fraud Confirmed
timeline.description: The CEO confirms they sent no such email. The attacker has spoofed the CEO's address.

timeline.title: Incident Reported
timeline.description: The phishing email is reported to IT security and finance governance teams.

timeline.title: Controls Reviewed
timeline.description: Finance processes are updated to mandate dual approval and out-of-band verification for all large transfers.

node-1.title: Urgent CEO Wire Transfer Request
node-1.situation: The CFO receives an email from what appears to be the CEO's personal email address. It requests an urgent, confidential wire transfer of $180,000 to a new supplier, emphasising the deal must close today and that normal procedures should be skipped to maintain secrecy.
node-1.question: How should the CFO respond?

options.label: A
options.text: Do not initiate any transfer. Contact the CEO directly using a known phone number or in person to verify the request.

options.label: B
options.text: Process the transfer immediately — the urgency and secrecy suggest a legitimate sensitive deal.

options.label: C
options.text: Reply to the email asking for more details before transferring funds.

node-2.title: CEO Confirms No Request Sent
node-2.situation: The CEO confirms they sent no such request. The attacker used a look-alike personal domain to impersonate them. The email has been forwarded to IT security.
node-2.question: What immediate action should IT security take?

options.label: A
options.text: Block the spoofed domain, analyse the email headers, and check whether any other executives received similar emails.

options.label: B
options.text: Delete the email and take no further action since no money was transferred.

node-3.title: Broader Executive Targeting Discovered
node-3.situation: IT finds two more executives received similar emails over the past week. The attacker appears to have researched leadership structure using the company website and press releases.
node-3.question: What organisational control should be strengthened to prevent future whaling attacks?

options.label: A
options.text: Implement a mandatory out-of-band phone verification policy for any financial transfer request received via email, regardless of apparent sender.

options.label: B
options.text: Remove all executive names and roles from the company website to prevent OSINT.

node-4.title: Email Security Hardening
node-4.situation: IT recommends adding a clear [EXTERNAL] email banner and configuring email gateway rules to flag emails where the display name matches an internal executive but the sending domain is external.
node-4.question: Should this control be implemented?

options.label: A
options.text: Yes — implement display-name spoofing detection and external email banners across the organisation immediately.

options.label: B
options.text: No — staff should already be able to distinguish internal from external emails without a banner.

node-5.title: Scenario Complete
node-5.situation: No funds were transferred. Spoofed domains have been blocked, out-of-band verification has been mandated for financial requests, and display-name spoofing detection is now active. Executive awareness briefings have been scheduled.
node-5.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
